name: Einsteinium
name_origin: Named in honor of Albert Einstein, the theoretical physicist.

discovery.discoverer: Albert Ghiorso and team at Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory; identified in fallout from the 'Ivy Mike' thermonuclear test

occurrence.abundance_summary: Synthetic element not found in nature; produced in microgram quantities by prolonged neutron irradiation of plutonium, curium, and berkelium targets at the High Flux Isotope Reactor.

compounds.name: Einsteinium(III) oxide
compounds.summary: Pale solid that glows due to intense self-radiation; the most studied einsteinium compound, prepared in submilligram amounts.

compounds.name: Einsteinium(III) chloride
compounds.summary: Yellow crystalline solid used in studies of the +3 actinides; one of the few einsteinium compounds for which structural data exist.

compounds.name: Einsteinium(III) iodide
compounds.summary: Amber crystalline solid notable for being one of the few transcurium compounds whose crystal structure has been determined.

uses: Scientific research into the chemistry of heavy actinides and relativistic effects in heavy elements
Target material for the synthesis of heavier elements, including mendelevium
reactivity_summary: A silvery metal that, like the other heavy actinides, reacts with air, steam, and acids. Einsteinium emits enough heat from self-radiation that it visibly glows. Its chemistry is dominated by the +3 state, but the +2 oxidation state is more accessible than in lighter actinides, and a +4 state has been reported in solid compounds.
isotopes_summary: All isotopes are radioactive. Einsteinium-252 has the longest half-life (471.7 days), but Es-253 (half-life 20.47 days) is the most commonly produced isotope in reactor irradiation campaigns and is used in heavier-element synthesis experiments.

citations.source: IUPAC Periodic Table of the Elements
Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory - Einsteinium chemistry research
